user_prompt: Map the data flows for our SaaS: signup → CRM → email tool → support tool. Identify each processor.
actual_output:
**Data Flow Mapping & Processor Inventory — SaaS Platform** *Disclaimer: This is GDPR compliance guidance, not legal advice. Specific tool names, data categories, and transfer mechanisms must be validated by your team and, where appropriate, qualified legal counsel or a DPO.* ### 1. High-Level Data Flow Map ```mermaid graph TD A[EU Data Subject] -->|1. Signup
name, email, account details, IP, consent signals| B[Signup Form / SaaS Website
(Controller)] B -->|2. Storage & primary processing| C[Controller's Backend / Database] C -->|3. Sync / API push
(name, email, company, usage metadata)| D[CRM
(Processor 1)] D -->|4. Trigger / segment
(email address, name, campaign data)| E[Email Tool
(Processor 2)] D -->|5. Ticket creation / lookup
(name, email, conversation history, account data)| F[Support Tool
(Processor 3)] C -->|Direct integration possible| F E -->|Transactional / marketing emails| A F -->|Support responses| A ``` **Key Data Categories Flowing Through the Chain:** - **Basic identifiers:** Name, email address - **Account data:** Subscription status, plan type, signup date - **Behavioral / usage metadata:** (if synced to CRM or support tool) - **Support content:** Tickets, chat logs, attachments (may contain special category data if users volunteer it) - **Technical data:** IP address, user agent, timestamps (often logged at signup and in support tool) ### 2. Processor Identification (Art. 4(8) & Art. 28) | Stage | Role | Typical Processors | GDPR Obligations | Current Compliance Gap (if any) | |-------|------|--------------------|------------------|---------------------------------| | **Signup / Website** | Controller + possible Processor | Your hosting provider (e.g. AWS, GCP, Azure, Vercel), authentication service (e.g. Auth0, Firebase) | Art. 28 DPA required if they process personal data on your behalf | Confirm which sub-processors are used for the signup flow | | **CRM** | Processor | HubSpot, Salesforce, Pipedrive, Brevo CRM, etc. | Must have Art. 28 DPA + SCCs if international transfer. Processor may not process data for its own purposes. | DPA must be in place and cover all processing purposes | | **Email Tool** | Processor (often sub-processor of CRM) | Mailchimp (Intuit), SendGrid (Twilio), Brevo, Customer.io, Klaviyo | Art. 28 DPA + clear instructions on permitted uses. Must maintain records (Art. 30). Many have sub-processors (e.g. cloud hosting). | Need visibility into sub-processors and their safeguards | | **Support Tool** | Processor | Zendesk, Intercom, Gorgias, Freshdesk, Help Scout | Art. 28 DPA required. Often processes data on a “controller-to-processor” basis for support tickets. May also act as independent controller for its own analytics. | Check whether tool is configured to minimize data shared and whether DPA covers all data types (including attachments) | ### 3. Key GDPR Compliance Observations **Critical / High-Risk Items** - **Missing or incomplete DPAs (Art. 28(3))**: Every arrow from your organisation to a third-party tool above requires a binding data processing agreement containing the mandatory elements (processing instructions, security measures, assistance with DSARs, deletion obligations, sub-processor notification, etc.). - **International Transfers (Art. 44–50)**: Most popular CRM, email, and support tools are based in the United States or use US-based sub-processors. You must have valid transfer tools in place (2021 EU SCCs + Transfer Impact Assessment (TIA) or Binding Corporate Rules). Failure here is one of the highest fine-risk areas post-Schrems II. - **Record of Processing Activities (Art. 30)**: You must maintain (and keep updated) a register showing each of these flows, the categories of data subjects, purposes, processors, transfer mechanisms, and retention periods. **Medium-Risk Items** - **Purpose limitation & data minimisation (Art. 5(1)(b)–(c))**: Ensure the CRM → Email Tool and CRM → Support Tool flows only send the fields strictly necessary for the stated purpose. Over-syncing of usage analytics or full ticket history can create unnecessary risk. - **Sub-processor transparency (Art. 28(2))**: Email and support tools routinely use further sub-processors. You must receive notice of new sub-processors and have objection rights. - **Support tool special-category risk**: If customers include health, religious, or other special-category data in support tickets, additional safeguards (Art.
judge_reasoning: The chatbot provided a detailed data flow map, identified the processors involved, and cited relevant GDPR articles as requested by the user.
